does: This paper reverse-engineers the algorithm learned by small transformers trained on modular addition, discovering that networks learn to use discrete Fourier transforms and trigonometric identities to convert addition into rotation operations on a circle. The authors fully mechanistically interpret the learned behavior by analyzing activations, weights, and performing targeted ablations in Fourier space. Based on this understanding, they define progress measures—continuous metrics that track network behavior—which reveal three distinct training phases: memorization, circuit formation, and generalization. These progress measures make seemingly discontinuous emergent phenomena like grokking (sudden generalization after prolonged memorization) tractable as continuous dynamical processes, allowing precise characterization of how and when the network transitions between qualitatively different computational modes.
connects: This work builds on recent mechanistic interpretability efforts to understand neural network internals by decomposing learned computations into interpretable components, extending methods previously applied to smaller models and simpler tasks. It directly investigates the grokking phenomenon, a recently discovered empirical fact where networks generalize abruptly after long periods of memorization, which challenges standard understanding of learning dynamics. The paper connects to broader research on emergence in neural networks and seeks to ground discontinuous behavioral changes in continuous underlying processes, drawing on dynamical systems perspectives. It also leverages Fourier analysis and group theory, classical tools in signal processing and mathematics, applying them to understand modern deep learning, thus bridging classical interpretability techniques with transformer-based models.
enables: This work enables future mechanistic interpretability studies by demonstrating that even complex emergent phenomena can be fully decoded and expressed as continuous progress measures, providing a template for understanding other forms of grokking or sudden capability gains. The methodology of using mechanistic understanding to construct progress measures could apply to larger models and more complex tasks, potentially offering interpretable indicators for when neural networks will suddenly improve. The findings suggest that many apparently discontinuous phase transitions in learning may decompose into continuous underlying processes, opening avenues for predicting and controlling emergent capabilities. This approach could inform AI safety research by enabling earlier detection of dangerous capability shifts and validation that learned algorithms match intended behaviors before deploying systems.
